coredata.dc:title: Synergistically enhanced activity of nitrogen-doped carbon dots/graphene composites for oxygen reduction reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Hui
Zhao, Qingshan
Liu, Jingyan
Ma, Xiao
Rao, Yuan
Shao, Xiaodong
Li, Zhongtao
Wu, Wenting
Ning, Hui
Wu, Mingbo
coredata.dc:description: With rapid dissociative adsorption of oxygen, nitrogen-doped carbon nanomaterials have been demonstrated to be efficient alternative catalysts for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in fuel cells. Herein, we developed a mild hydrothermal strategy to construct nitrogen-doped carbon dots/graphene (NCDs-NG) composites towards ORR. Carbon dots (CDs) were derived from petroleum coke via acid oxidation while graphene oxide (GO) was obtained from graphite by modified Hummer’s method. Graphene was employed as a conductive substrate to disperse CDs during hydrothermal reducing reaction while ammonia was utilized as N source to dope both graphene and CDs. The synergistic effects, i.e. CDs as pillars for graphene and catalytic sites for ORR, the high conductivity of graphene, the quick O2 adsorption on doped pyridinic nitrogen endow the NCDs-NG composites with enhanced ORR catalytic performance in alkaline electrolyte. The onset potential of −95mV and kinetic current density of 12.7mAcm−2 at −0.7V (vs. Ag/AgCl) can be compared to those of the commercial 20wt% Pt/C catalyst. The electron transfer number is about 3.9, revealing a four-electron pathway for ORR. The optimal NCDs-NG catalyst shows superior durability and methanol tolerance than 20wt% Pt/C. This work demonstrates a feasible and effective strategy to prepare metal-free efficient ORR electrocatalysts for fuel cell applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Oxygen reduction reaction
Electrocatalysts
Nitrogen doping
Carbon dots
Petroleum coke

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the preparation of NCDs-NG composites.
(a) SEM image, (b) EDS elemental mapping, (c) TEM image and (d) High-resolution TEM image of the NCDs-NG-12 composite. (Inset) High-resolution TEM image showing lattice spacing of NCDs.
XPS spectra of NCDs-NG-12 composite: (a) wide spectrum; high-resolution (b) C1s, and (c) N1s XPS spectrum. (d) Raman spectra of the CDs, GO and NCDs-NG-12 samples.
FTIR spectra of the CDs, GO, the CDs and GO mixture, CDs-G and NCDs-NG-12 samples.
N insertion pathway in carbon during hydrothermal process.

(a) CVs of NCDs-NG-12 in N2- and O2-saturated 0.1M KOH solution at a scan rate of 50mVs−1. (b) LSVs of CDs, GO, CDs-G, NCDs, NG, NCDs-NG-8, NCDs-NG-12, NCDs-NG-16 and Pt/C in O2-saturated 0.1M KOH solution at a rotation rate of 1600rpm and a scan rate of 10mVs−1. (c) LSVs of NCDs-NG-12 in O2-saturated 0.1M KOH solution at various rotation speeds and a scan rate of 10mVs−1. (d) K-L plots of NCDs-NG-12 at −0.3, −0.4, −0.5, −0.6 and −0.7V.

(a) Chronoamperometric responses of NCDs-NG-12 and Pt/C catalysts in O2-saturated 0.1M KOH solution for 40000s. Chronoamperometric responses of NCDs-NG-12 and Pt/C catalysts in O2-saturated 0.1M KOH solution with addition of methanol (b) and CO (c) at 300s. (d) Electrocatalytic activity given as the kinetic current density at −0.7V for all samples.

head.ce:title: Synergistically enhanced activity of nitrogen-doped carbon dots/graphene composites for oxygen reduction reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, College of Chemical Engineering, China University of Petroleum, Qingdao 266580, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A mild hydrothermal strategy was developed to construct nitrogen-doped carbon dots/graphene (NCDs-NG) composites for ORR.

With rapid dissociative adsorption of oxygen, nitrogen-doped carbon nanomaterials have been demonstrated to be efficient alternative catalysts for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in fuel cells. Herein, we developed a mild hydrothermal strategy to construct nitrogen-doped carbon dots/graphene (NCDs-NG) composites towards ORR. Carbon dots (CDs) were derived from petroleum coke via acid oxidation while graphene oxide (GO) was obtained from graphite by modified Hummer’s method. Graphene was employed as a conductive substrate to disperse CDs during hydrothermal reducing reaction while ammonia was utilized as N source to dope both graphene and CDs. The synergistic effects, i.e. CDs as pillars for graphene and catalytic sites for ORR, the high conductivity of graphene, the quick O2 adsorption on doped pyridinic nitrogen endow the NCDs-NG composites with enhanced ORR catalytic performance in alkaline electrolyte. The onset potential of −95mV and kinetic current density of 12.7mAcm−2 at −0.7V (vs. Ag/AgCl) can be compared to those of the commercial 20wt% Pt/C catalyst. The electron transfer number is about 3.9, revealing a four-electron pathway for ORR. The optimal NCDs-NG catalyst shows superior durability and methanol tolerance than 20wt% Pt/C. This work demonstrates a feasible and effective strategy to prepare metal-free efficient ORR electrocatalysts for fuel cell applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Fuel cells, which can directly generate electricity via electrochemically reducing oxygen, have currently received great research interest due to its high energy conversion efficiency, virtually no pollution, and potential large-scale applications [1–4]. However, the performance of fuel cells greatly depends on the cathodic oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) on account of its sluggish reaction kinetics [3]. Up to now, the most active electrocatalysts for ORR are metal-based catalysts, especially platinum (Pt). Unfortunately, their commercial applications are severely restricted by limited resources, high cost, CO deactivation and poor durability, etc [5,6].

Currently, metal-free carbon materials, such as graphene [7–9], carbon nanotubes [10–12], and porous carbons [13,14], have been paid much attention as promising alternative catalysts for ORR. Compared to metal-based catalysts, carbon materials not only provide higher electronic conductivity, but also possess the advantages of low cost, long durability and environmental friendly [15–17]. In the carbon material family, the zero-dimensional (0D) carbon dots (CDs), have aroused intensive interest due to their exceptional advantages such as high aqueous solubility [18], brilliant biocompatibility [19–21] and so on [22,23]. Nevertheless, CDs tend to aggregation, which is unfavorable for the sufficient exposure of the active sites [24], thus the electron transfer is easily restricted. Therefore, new strategies to well disperse CDs are necessary. Graphene has been widely employed as an ideal substrate in electrochemical fields due to their superior electrical conductivity, two-dimensional structure and large surface area [25–28]. Accordingly, combining CDs with graphene together is expected to synergistically enhance the electrocatalytic ORR activity.

Heteroatom doping is the most commonly used method to adjust the properties of carbon materials, which can effectively tune their intrinsic properties, including electronic characteristics, surface and local chemical features [13,29–34]. Recent researches indicate that nitrogen doping of carbon materials seems to be a promising method for a promoted effect towards ORR. For instance, Qu et al. [35] synthesized N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) by cyclic voltammogram (CV) scanning. The as-produced N-GQDs with a N/C atomic ratio of ca. 4.3% exhibited superior electrocatalytic ability for ORR. Lee et al. [36] demonstrated a robust hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) from the unripe peach for fluorescent bioimaging and electrocatalytic ORR. Similarly, nitrogen doped graphene (NG), which were synthesized through different methods including chemical vapor deposition [37], plasma treatment [38], and thermal annealing with NH3 [39], have been widely applied as ORR catalysts. However, rigorous conditions or special equipment are often required in these process. Therefore, it still remains a challenge to develop metal-free catalysts with high activity, low cost, strong durability and under mild conditions.

Herein, we developed a novel metal-free nitrogen-doped carbon dots/graphene (NCDs-NG) composite by hydrothermal self-assembly, using CDs, graphene oxide (GO) and ammonia as precursors. Ammonia is a kind of water-soluble solution that can decompose into NH3 and H2O. NH3 can react with oxygen-containing species to form intermediate, possibly amide-like species in the initial stage. With higher temperatures these species are subsequently transformed into more stable heterocyclic aromatic moieties in carbon materials. NCDs are uniformly distributed on the NG sheet through the abundant functional groups. The as-prepared NCDs-NG composites exhibit excellent ORR catalytic activity and stability compared to those of the commercial 20wt% Pt/C catalyst. The present strategy could inspire the development of metal-free carbon materials as electrocatalysts for various applications.
Synthesis of GO and CDs. GO was synthesized from graphite powder using the modified Hummer’s method [40]. CDs were synthesized from petroleum coke using our published procedure [41].

10.0mL 6.0mg/mL of CDs solution was added into the 10.0mL of 3.0mg/mL GO solution (the mass ratio of CDs and GO is 2:1), followed by addition of 1mL ammonia with the assistance of sonication, and further stirred for 2h. Then the mixture was sealed into a 100mL Teflon-lined autoclave and hydrothermal treated at 180°С for different time. Finally, the product was washed three times with deionized water and freeze-dried to obtain NCDs-NG. The samples treated for 8h, 12h and 16h are denoted as NCDs-NG-8, NCDs-NG-12 and NCDs-NG-16, respectively. To study the effect of ammonia, CDs-G was prepared using the same procedure except no N sources were provided. NCDs and NG were prepared using the same procedure except no GO and CDs added during the hydrothermal reaction, respectively. In addition, the concentration of the CDs was tuned to 3.0 and 9.0mg/mL to investigate the effect between CDs and graphene.

Morphologies of all samples were observed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4800, Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20, USA). Raman spectra were recorded on Renishaw RM 2000 spectrometer with an excitation wavelength of 514nm. FT-IR spectra were measured on a pressed KBr pellet employing Nicolet 6700 spectrometer. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained using a Thermol scientific Escalab 250Xi spectrometer with an Al-Kα Xray source.

Electrochemical measurements were conducted using 760E Bipotentiostat (CHI, Shanghai Chenhua) in a standard three-electrode cell. Electrocatalysts loaded on a glassy carbon disk (diameter: 4mm) were used as working electrode, a Pt column as counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl as reference electrode. All the potentials were valued versus Ag/AgCl and were not iR-corrected. Typically, 4mg catalysts mixed with 10μL 5wt% Nafion solution was dispersed in 2mL of 1:1v/v water/ethanol solution and ultrasonicated to form a homogeneous ink. 5μL catalyst ink was sequentially dropped onto the glassy carbon electrode. Working electrode of 20wt% commercial Pt/C (Johnson Matthey) was prepared in a similar manner. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were performed at a scan rate of 50mVs−1 in 0.1M KOH solution saturated with N2 or O2. Linear-sweep voltammetry (LSV) measurements were conducted at a scan rate of 10mVs−1 under different electrode rotation rates (400, 900, 1200, 1600 and 2000rpm). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed in O2-saturated 0.1M KOH solution from 10 KHz to 0.1Hz. Chronoamperometric test was measured at a fixed potential of −0.3V in O2- saturated 0.1M KOH solution for 40000s at a rotation rate of 1000rpm. For the methanol crossover and CO poisoning study, the current was record at −0.3V vs. Ag/AgCl with a rotation rate of 1600rpm, with 5% (v/v) methanol and CO added into the O2-saturated electrolyte around 300s, respectively.

The kinetic parameters (n and jK ) were calculated by Koutecky-Levich (K-L) Eqs. (1) and (2):
(1)
j−1 =jL −1 +jK −1 =(Bω1/2)−1 +jK −1
(2)
B=0.2nFC0(D0)2/3ν −1/6
Where j, jL and jK are the measured current density, the diffusion and kinetic limiting current densities, respectively, ω is the rotation rate in rpm, n is the electron transfer number, F is the Faraday constant (96485Cmol−1), C0 is the concentration of O2 (1.2×10−3 molL−1 in 0.1M KOH solution), D0 is the diffusion coefficient of O2 (1.9×10−5 cm2 s−1 in 0.1M KOH solution) and ν is the kinetic viscosity (0.01cm2 s−1 in 0.1M KOH solution).

The synthesis process of the NCDs-NG composites is illustrated in Scheme 1 (details are given in Experimental Section). During the hydrothermal self-assembly process, CDs can be uniformly attached to GO due to the strong interactions between the hydroxyl and carbonyl functional groups. Meanwhile, the NCDs-NG composites were eventually obtained in the presence of NH3·H2O. Fig. S1 shows a typical TEM image of the as-synthesized CDs with average diameter of 2–4nm.

The morphology of the NCDs-NG-12 composite was examined by SEM and TEM. The SEM image (Fig. 1 (a)) clearly shows a uniform laminar structure, which is similar to the pristine graphene sheets. Additionally, elemental dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping of NCDs-NG-12 (Fig. 1(b)) displays a uniform distribution of the N element, indicating the successful introduction of the N atoms into the carbon skeleton. As shown in TEM images of NCDs-NG-12 (Figs. 1(c) and S2(a)), it is clearly that a batch of NCDs are decorated on the surface of NG. Fig. 1(d) shows the high-resolution TEM image of NCDs, in which the lattice spacing of 0.21nm corresponds to the (100) in-plane lattice spacing of graphite.

The mass ratio of CDs to GO was considered as a crucial factor for dispersing CDs uniformly on the surface graphene and providing more active sites towards ORR. When the CDs/GO ratio was decreased to 1:1 (the concentration of CDs was 3.0mg/mL), there are no obvious variation compared to the graphene sheets (Fig. S2(b)). However, when the ratio was increased to 3:1 (the concentration of CDs was 9.0mg/mL), severe aggregation took place (Fig. S2(c)), which is not favorable to expose the ORR active sites of NCDs. This aggregation is probably due to the insufficient hydroxyl or carbonyl functional groups provided by GO to support the CDs. Herein, the ratio of 2:1 (the concentration of CDs was 6.0mg/mL) is considered to be the optimal proportion in this work.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to characterize the elementary composition and content of samples. Figs. 2 (a) and S3 reveal the presence of C, O, and N elements in all NCDs-NG composites with different hydrothermal time, indicating the effective N doping process using ammonia. The high resolution of C1s spectrum of NCDs-NG-12 is shown in Fig. 2(b). The peaks centered at 284.6, 285.1, 285.9, 286.9, and 288.4eV are attributed to sp2 C, CN, CO, CO, and OCO, respectively. Compared with CDs and GO (Fig. S4), the peaks intensity of CO decreased, which can be attributed to the reduction of oxygen groups in the hydrothermal process. The high-resolution XPS N1s spectra of all NCDs-NG composites (Figs. 2(c) and S5) could be deconvoluted into four types of nitrogen species [42]: pyridinic nitrogen (N-1, 398.7eV); amine or amide nitrogen (N-2, 399.5eV); pyrrolic nitrogen (N-3, 400.4eV), composed of pyrrol, lactam and pyridone functional groups (the pyridone functionality is the tautomeric form of the lactame functionality); quaternary nitrogen (N-4, 401.4eV), including protonated or graphitic nitrogen. Table S1 summarizes the content of four nitrogen species (N-1, N-2, N-3, N-4) in NCDs-NG samples. It is clear that the total N content increased continuously (from 6.3 at.% to 10.2 at.%) with prolonging the hydrothermal time and the intensity of N-1 is the most abundant species at 12h. In contrast, N-2 and N-3 are the main species when the hydrothermal time was 8h. It is speculated that the lactame and pyrrol functional groups convert partly to pyridine for a long time [42]. When the hydrothermal time increased from 12 to 16h, the massive increase of N-3 is due to the simultaneous decrease of N-1 and N-2. In addition, the amount of N-4 increase slightly may result from the surface nitrogen functionalities reassembling into graphite cluster during the heating process [42]. Such different amounts of N configurations in NCDs-NG composites will be shown to exert a significant influence on their ORR electrocatalytic activities (see below).

Raman test was further conducted to investigate carbon microstructures of NCDs-NG composites (Fig. 2(d)). Two distinct peaks are observed at ∼1590cm−1 and ∼1349cm−1 in NCDs-NG-12, which can be attributed to the existence of graphite sp2 carbon (G-band) and disordered sp3 carbon (D-band), respectively. The ratio of the D and G band intensities (ID/IG) in NCD-NG-12 is 1.20, which is larger than that of the CDs (0.98) and GO (0.80). The increase of the ID/IG value might be attributed to the addition of more surface defects and disordered structures in NCD-NG composite, which were caused by the insertion of N in the carbon skeleton. As a result, these defects and disordered structures could significantly improve the electrocatalytic performance of NCD-NG due to enhanced π-bonding and electron donor-acceptor characteristics [43].

To investigate the formation mechanism of the NCDs-NG composite, FTIR spectra of CDs, GO, CDs+GO mixture, CDs-G without N, and NCDs-NG-12 were compared. As shown in Fig. 3 , for CDs and GO, the peaks located at 3441–3330cm−1 and 1717cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching vibration of OH and CO, respectively, while peaks at 1608–1603cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of CC. In addition, the 1439–1215cm−1 peak is due to COH or CH stretching vibration, and the 1128–1047cm−1 peak can be assigned to CO stretching vibration, respectively. These characteristic absorption bands suggest that there are plenty of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs and GO. There was no obvious change in the FT-IR spectra of the CDs+GO mixture versus the CDs and GO samples, indicating oxygen-containing functional groups remained unchanged before the hydrothermal process. Nevertheless, the CO stretching vibration were blue-shifted in CDs-G, suggesting that CO groups were affected with electron-donating groups [44]. This variations may be attributed to the dehydration between the hydroxyl and carbonyl functional groups of CDs and GO during the hydrothermal process. In addition, strong absorption peaks of the CO and CO stretching vibration became obscured markedly, and a stronger absorption at 1375cm−1 attributed to the CN in-plane stretching vibrations appeared in NCDs-NG-12. All these indicate that plenty of oxygen functionalities on the surface of CDs and GO have been reduced and nitrogen was doped successfully during the hydrothermal process, which is consistent with the results of XPS analysis.

Based on the above analyses, the NCDs-NG composite was formed in two main steps: (1) the strong interactions between the hydroxyl and carbonyl functional groups of GO and CDs ensured the compact packing between them, assisting CDs well distributed on the surfaces of the GO; (2) the composite undergone reduction (the removal of −OH and −COO−) and N doped in the hydrothermal self-assembly process. During the heating process, NH3 reacts with carboxylic acid species to form intermediate, possibly amide-like, species [45]. With the increase of hydrothermal time, the introduced nitrogen tends to be present in different functional groups, such as pyridinic nitrogen, pyrrolic nitrogen, protonated and graphic nitrogen [42,45–47]. According the analysis on the structure by FT-IR and XPS, the N-doping pathway of the NCDs-NG composite in ammonia is depicted in Fig. 4 .

The ORR electrocatalytic activity of NCDs-NG was first investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements in N2 and O2-saturated 0.1M KOH solution. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), NCDs-NG-12 exhibited a featureless current response in N2-saturated solution. In strong contrast, a distinct cathodic peak at −0.24V was observed in O2-saturated solution, indicating pronounced ORR catalytic activity of the NCDs-NG composite.

To gain further insight into the ORR, linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) measurements were performed at a scan rate of 10mVs−1 in O2-saturated 0.1M KOH solution. To investigate the role of nitrogen, ORR activities of the CDs, GO, CDs-G, NCDs, NG, NCDs-NG-8, and NCDs-NG-16 were also studied. As a control, the performance of commercial 20wt% Pt/C was also studied. From Fig. 5(b), the onset potential of CDs, GO, CDs-G, NCDs, NG, NCDs-NG-8, NCDs-NG-12, NCDs-NG-16 and Pt/C was −390, −282, −225, −287, −150, −100, −95, −105 and −45mV, respectively. Remarkably, all the NCDs-NG catalyst exhibit enhanced activity towards the ORR compared with the NCDs and NG catalysts, which is attributed to the synergistic effect between CDs and GO [48]. Also, except for CDs, GO, CDs-G, which had a two-stage characterized ORR process, all other N-doping samples showed the typical one-stage pathway, indicating the better ORR performance induced by the doping with N atoms which could modify the charge density on the electrocatalyst and weaken the adsorption of reaction intermediates (O and OOH) [49]. Meanwhile, it was clear that the ORR activity was significantly influenced by heating time, with NCDs- NG-12 having the most positive onset potential and largest current density. The relatively inferior ORR activities of NCDs-NG-8 and NCDs-NG-16 could be ascribed to the relative insufficient pyridinic nitrogen (Table S1). As reported before, an increase in pyridinic nitrogen will result in chemically active, localized areas of higher electron density, which could improve electrical conductivity and electron transfer rate [50]. The existence of more pyridinic nitrogen in NCDs-NG-12 can facilitate O2 adsorption at the initial step of the ORR. In contrast, the existence of less pyridinic- and more pyrrolic- nitrogen in the other two samples would decrease the electrical conductivity, as revealed by the impedance measurements (Fig. S6), resulting in the lower ORR activity. Therefore, it demonstrates that the hydrothermal time exert a large influence on the ORR activity of NCDs-NG catalysts. The appropriate time could guarantee the high content of active nitrogen configurations for enhanced ORR activity.

Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves of the ORR on the NCDs-NG-12 electrode at various rotating speeds are reported in Fig. 5(c). The current densities show a typical increase with larger rotating speeds due to the shortened diffusion length at higher speeds [30]. To further study the ORR kinetics of the catalyst, the calculated Koutecky-Levich (K-L) plots at different potentials are shown in Fig. 5(d). Obviously, all the curves exhibit good linearity, implying a first-order reaction with respect to dissolved oxygen. The transferred electron number (n) was estimated to be 3.5-3.9 over potentials ranging from −0.3 to −0.7V, suggesting a four-electron process for the ORR on the NCDs-NG catalyst.

It is well known that the electrochemical stability and tolerance towards methanol crossover and CO poisoning are important for ORR electrocatalysts. Therefore, chronoamperometric responses were performed for both NCDs-NG-12 and Pt/C electrodes in O2-saturated 0.1M KOH solution at −0.3V. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), current density of NCDs-NG-12 still remains 88% after 40000 s, whereas the commercial Pt/C decreased to 70%, indicating that the NCDs-NG have a much better stability than the commercial Pt/C catalyst towards ORR in alkaline medium. The methanol tolerance and CO poisoning effect were also evaluated by chronoamperometry in O2-saturated 0.1M KOH solution after the addition of methanol or CO at time 300s. The chronoamperometric responses (Fig. 6(b)) show the current density of the commercial Pt/C electrocatalyst decreases obviously, while NCDs-NG-12 shows a negligible reduction, indicating an excellent immunity toward methanol crossover of the as-prepared NCDs-NG electrocatalysts. In Fig. 6(c), it also can be found that the electrocatalytic current density of the commercial Pt/C catalyst is abruptly reduced for an approximately 72% current loss, whereas NCDs-NG-12 exhibits a relatively mild attenuation with a high current retention (64%). It indicates that the resistance of the NCDs-NG to CO poisoning is much better than that of the commercial Pt/C catalyst towards ORR.

LSVs at different rotating speeds for the other samples (CDs, GO, CDs-G, NCDs, NG, NCDs-NG-8, NCDs-NG-16, Pt/C) are in Fig. S7. Fig. 6(d) summarizes n and kinetic current densities (jK) obtained from the K-L equation at −0.7V for all samples. For 20wt% Pt/C, the electron transfer number was 4.0 and the kinetic current density was 14.1mAcm−2. NCDs-NG-12 with its nearly full four-electron ORR process (n=3.9) and large kinetic current (jK =12.7mAcm−2) outperformed NCDs-NG-16 (n=3.2, jK =10.5mAcm−2), NCDs-NG-8 (n=2.7, jK =9.5mAcm−2), NG (n=2.8, jK =5.7mAcm−2), NCDs (n=3.0, jK =2.5mAcm−2), CDs-G (n=2.9, jK =4.8mAcm−2), GO (n=2.7, jK =3.2mAcm−2), CDs (n=2.3, jK =1.6mAcm−2), highlighting the importance of tuning doping time and the synergistic effects between two carbon materials.

The above structural and compositional characterizations have confirmed that the NCDs-NG composite has been prepared successfully by the hydrothermal treatment in the presence of ammonia. The outstanding electrocatalytic ORR activities of NCDs-NG-12 could be ascribed to the following reasons. Firstly, CDs can insert between the graphene sheets to prevent graphene sheets from restacking and accelerate mass tranfer. Secondly, graphene has been employed as a conductive substrate to disperse CDs for avoiding their aggregation and providing abundant active sites. Thirdly, the relative abundant pyridinic nitrogen in carbon skeleton can promote O2 adsorption, which further facilitating ORR activity.

In summary, The NCDs-NG composite was prepared successfully from petroleum coke-based CDs and GO via an efficient hydrothermal process in ammonia. As a metal-free ORR catalyst, the performance of the NCDs-NG can be compared to that of the commercial 20wt% Pt/C catalyst with respect to the onset potential (−95mV) and large kinetic current density (12.7mAcm−2). Simultaneously, the catalyst exhibits superior methanol tolerance and stability relative to the Pt/C catalyst. This outstanding activity is attributed to the favorable synergistic effect among CDs, graphene, and N-doping. This study offers a novel strategy to construct low-cost and metal-free ORR electrocatalysts as promising candidates for electrochemical energy devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51372277, 51572296, U1662113); the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (15CX08005A, 17CX06029); the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2016M592268); the Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (ZR2016BB18).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2017.06.225.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Oxygen electrochemistry as a cornerstone for sustainable energy conversion
Metal-free catalysts for oxygen reduction reaction
Recent progress of carbonaceous materials in fuel cell applications: An overview
What are batteries fuel cells, and supercapacitors?
Facile synthesis of mesoporous nitrogen-doped graphene: an efficient methanol-tolerant cathodic catalyst for oxygen reduction reaction
Edge-rich and dopant-free graphene as a highly efficient metal-free electrocatalyst for the oxygen reduction reaction
Current status, key challenges and its solutions in the design and development of graphene based ORR catalysts for the microbial fuel cell applications
Non-nitrogen doped and non-metal oxygen reduction electrocatalysts based on carbon nanotubes: mechanism and origin of ORR activity
Nitrogen-doped carbon nanotube aerogels for high-performance ORR catalysts
Chemically drilling carbon nanotubes for electrocatalytic oxygen reduction reaction
A metal-free bifunctional electrocatalyst for oxygen reduction and oxygen evolution reactions
Synergistic effect of Nitrogen-doped hierarchical porous carbon/graphene with enhanced catalytic performance for oxygen reduction reaction
Heterogeneous nanocarbon materials for oxygen reduction reaction
Acidically oxidized carbon cloth: a novel metal-free oxygen evolution electrode with high catalytic activity
A green and template recyclable approach to prepare Fe3O4/porous carbon from petroleum asphalt for lithium-ion batteries
Cu-N dopants boost electron transfer and photooxidation reactions of carbon dots
Carbon-dot-decorated carbon nitride nanoparticles for enhanced photodynamic therapy against hypoxic tumor via water splitting
One-pot to synthesize multifunctional carbon dots for near infrared fluorescence imaging and photothermal cancer therapy
Facile synthesis of fluorescent graphene quantum dots from coffee grounds for bioimaging and sensing
Graphene quantum dots: an emerging material for energy-related applications and beyond
Solvothermal conversion of coal into nitrogen-doped carbon dots with singlet oxygen generation and high quantum yield
Synergistically enhanced activity of graphene quantum dot/multi-walled carbon nanotube composites as metal-free catalysts for oxygen reduction reaction
Co3O4 nanocrystals on graphene as a synergistic catalyst for oxygen reduction reaction
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots decorated on graphene: a novel all-carbon hybrid electrocatalyst for enhanced oxygen reduction reaction
Supramolecular gel-assisted synthesis Co2P particles anchored in multielement co-doped graphene as efficient bifunctional electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction and evolution
Boron- and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots/graphene hybrid nanoplatelets as efficient electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction
Simple one-step synthesis of fluorine-doped carbon nanoparticles as potential alternative metal-free electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction reaction
Heteroatom-doped graphene-based materials for energy-relevant electrocatalytic processes
Nitrogen and sulfur co-doping of partially exfoliated MWCNTs as Three Dimensional structured electrocatalyst for the oxygen reduction reaction
